title: Homer Simpson This Is Your Wife

jokes.text: Why the hell would he want to do that....blessed sacrament that has made my life so rich?
jokes.setup: Homer starts to criticize marriage
jokes.punchline: Abrupt pivot to praising it as a blessed sacrament
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer mid-sentence reversal. Well-structured misdirection with perfect character voice.

jokes.text: I like your hat, sweetie. / I'm not wearing a hat. / I mean, the one at the house.
jokes.setup: Homer compliments Marge's hat
jokes.punchline: She's not wearing one; he means some random hat at home
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective Homer obliviousness. Good economy but fairly predictable.

jokes.text: Another party and we cain't go. Yeah. Just 'cause we's afraid of using the uppity box.
jokes.setup: Rural characters can't go to party
jokes.punchline: 'Uppity box' as hillbilly term for elevator
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character-specific language. 'Uppity box' is genuinely creative wordplay that feels authentic to the characters.

jokes.text: Ain't no party like a Lenny party 'cause a Lenny party don't stop.
jokes.setup: Reference to Coolio's 'Ain't No Party Like a West Coast Party'
jokes.punchline: Applied to mundane Lenny from the nuclear plant
jokes.explanation: Pop culture reference with minimal twist. The juxtaposition is mildly funny but relies on recognition rather than craft.

jokes.text: I got to work tomorrow. I better go. My god. How did Lenny get to be newsfake magazine's man of the year?
jokes.setup: Ned sees what appears to be a prestigious award
jokes.punchline: It's revealed to be fake/parody magazine
jokes.explanation: Good visual misdirection joke. Ned's gullibility is well-established character trait.

jokes.text: That's just a souvenir from an amusement park. / What? / Next you'll be telling me he didn't meet Woody Woodpecker.
jokes.setup: Homer explains the fake award is just a souvenir
jokes.punchline: Ned's naive belief extends to cartoon characters being real
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of Ned's naivety. The Woody Woodpecker detail is absurd but character-consistent.

jokes.text: I dated the woman in that suit for three months. Then she left me for the guy who cleans the vomit off the roller coasters.
jokes.setup: Lenny had a relationship with someone in a character costume
jokes.punchline: She left him for an even more degrading job
jokes.explanation: Excellently dark and specific. The 'vomit cleaner' detail is perfectly degrading escalation that fits Lenny's loser persona.

jokes.text: Hey, Lenny, I see you cut the celery at an angle here. Makes my straight-cut celery look like crap.
jokes.setup: Homer notices Lenny's food preparation technique
jokes.punchline: He's genuinely threatened by superior celery cutting
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer pettiness over something trivial. The specificity of 'angle-cut vs straight-cut' celery is nicely absurd.

jokes.text: Attention, everybody! Please shut up! I know you're all wondering why you're here. The fact is, I'm dying... ...to tell you that I have adopted... ...a new faith... ...in the power of technological advances to make me happy.
jokes.setup: Dramatic pause suggests Lenny is literally dying
jokes.punchline: Multiple misdirections lead to announcing a new TV
jokes.explanation: Well-structured triple misdirection, though somewhat verbose. The religious language for a TV purchase is good satire.

jokes.text: I've never seen a picture so highly defined!
jokes.setup: Character reacts to HD TV
jokes.punchline: Literal interpretation of 'high definition'
jokes.explanation: Weak wordplay that doesn't really work. The pun on 'highly defined' is forced and obvious.

jokes.text: Lenny, this tv is amazing. If you're not careful, I might just spend the rest of my life on your couch. / You don't mean that literally, of course. / You're right, Lenny. I...
jokes.setup: Homer jokes about living on Lenny's couch
jokes.punchline: Lenny's concern and Homer's pause suggests he does mean it literally
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Homer's later behavior. The awkward pause after 'You're right, Lenny. I...' is perfect character work.

jokes.text: Lenny, bring me a beer and your deepest chamber pot. Chop-chop.
jokes.setup: Homer has become completely comfortable at Lenny's
jokes.punchline: Demands increasingly degrading services including a chamber pot
jokes.explanation: 'Chamber pot' is an absurdly archaic term that makes Homer's laziness even more ridiculous. Good escalation from previous scene.

jokes.text: You can see the soulless emptiness in that shark's eyes.
jokes.setup: Homer comments on the HD picture quality showing a shark
jokes.punchline: Philosophical observation about a predator's soullessness
jokes.explanation: Sets up the next joke nicely. Homer's pseudo-philosophical observation is mildly funny but mainly serves as setup.

jokes.text: Two and a half men. You can see the soulless emptiness in Charlie Sheen's eyes.
jokes.setup: Previous comment about shark's soulless eyes
jokes.punchline: Same observation applied to Charlie Sheen
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback structure with perfect economy. The Charlie Sheen dig is both topical and savage, elevated by the callback format.

jokes.text: Dad, please come home. We miss you. / Is that a high-def tv?! / Mom didn't say anything about high-def!
jokes.setup: Kids make emotional plea for Homer to come home
jokes.punchline: Bart is immediately distracted by the TV technology
jokes.explanation: Good character-based misdirection. Bart's priorities are perfectly wrong, and the 'Mom didn't say anything about high-def' adds another layer.

jokes.text: Later, if I have enough energy, we can walk up to the tv and I'll show you how thin it is. / Wow, there's a rainbow outside. / Oh, yeah? Well, right there's a commercial with a dancing cold sore.
jokes.setup: Homer is too lazy to demonstrate the TV thickness; Lisa points out natural beauty
jokes.punchline: Homer counters rainbow with grotesque TV commercial
jokes.explanation: Brilliant juxtaposition of natural wonder vs. gross commercialism. 'Dancing cold sore' is perfectly disgusting and specific.

jokes.text: Break out! Ha-ha! Hoo-hoo! Ha-ha! Break out! Ha-ha! Hoo-hoo! Ha-ha! Ha! Hoo-hoo! He-he-ha! Ha-ha!
jokes.setup: Homer is presumably dancing or reacting to the cold sore commercial
jokes.punchline: Extended sequence of maniacal laughter/celebration
jokes.explanation: Visual gag that doesn't translate well to transcript. Likely funny in animation but verbose and unclear in text form.

jokes.text: And bodell jenks the third crashes into bodell jenks! The winner is Bodell Jenks, junior!
jokes.setup: Racing commentary with confusing similar names
jokes.punchline: Three generations of Bodell Jenks in the same race
jokes.explanation: Absurdist naming confusion is mildly funny but feels like background gag rather than focused joke.

jokes.text: wow, I can actually feel the heat. / Beat it. This is my alone time.
jokes.setup: Bart is impressed by the TV's realism
jokes.punchline: Homer kicks him out for 'alone time' with the TV
jokes.explanation: 'Alone time' suggests inappropriate relationship with the TV. Good escalation of Homer's obsession.

jokes.text: Can you at least take a picture of me with the tv? / I ain't fallin' for that again. / Yeah. Good times.
jokes.setup: Bart wants a picture with the TV
jokes.punchline: Homer implies there was a previous incident; awkward agreement about 'good times'
jokes.explanation: Dark implication about what happened last time, with uncomfortable father-son 'good times' moment.

jokes.text: Stupid non-plasma tv. Picture so blurry. Might as well rub dirt in my eyes.
jokes.setup: Homer is back home with the regular TV
jokes.punchline: Extreme physical metaphor for poor picture quality
jokes.explanation: 'Might as well rub dirt in my eyes' is a good absurd escalation of Homer's TV snobbery.

jokes.text: Marge! I love you! I love you! I love you!
jokes.setup: Marge mentions entering a contest for a plasma TV
jokes.punchline: Homer's love is entirely conditional on getting a TV
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer selfishness. The triple repetition shows his fake enthusiasm.

jokes.text: Now i really hope we win that contest. / There's a chance we won't win?!
jokes.setup: Marge expresses hope about winning
jokes.punchline: Homer is shocked that contests aren't guaranteed
jokes.explanation: Homer's complete misunderstanding of how contests work is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: My collection is complete. 40 years of the family circus.
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy announces completion of a collection
jokes.punchline: It's of Family Circus, a notoriously unfunny newspaper comic
jokes.explanation: Perfect Comic Book Guy obsessiveness applied to something universally mocked. Great comedy insider reference.

jokes.text: Congratulations, Mr. Simpson. You've won. / The big screen tv? / No. No one wins that. You won third prize, a tour of the FOX network in los angeles, california.
jokes.setup: Homer thinks he won the TV prize
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation that the advertised prize is never awarded; wins FOX tour instead
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta joke about contest scams and FOX self-deprecation. 'No one wins that' is perfectly cynical.

jokes.text: All expenses paid? / Look, I gotta go.
jokes.setup: Homer asks about expenses
jokes.punchline: Official hangs up rather than answer
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to previous joke. The abrupt hang-up implies expenses definitely not paid.

jokes.text: Here's where we make the best damn sports show, period. And there's where we make 'the worst damn comedy show, period.'
jokes.setup: Tour guide points out sports show production
jokes.punchline: Immediately admits to making terrible comedy
jokes.explanation: Good FOX self-deprecation. The parallel structure ('best damn'/'worst damn') works well.

jokes.text: why look. There's Dan Castellaneta from the Tracey Ullman show.
jokes.setup: Tour guide points out voice actor
jokes.punchline: References Simpsons' origins on Tracey Ullman, not the show itself
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta joke acknowledging the show's own history while pretending not to recognize its current success.

jokes.text: Hey, funny man! Say something funny! / Please don't lean out of the tram, sir. You might get hurt. / Don't lean out of the tram...
jokes.setup: Homer asks Dan Castellaneta to be funny
jokes.punchline: He gives boring safety instructions instead
jokes.explanation: Perfect meta joke where Homer's voice actor plays against type. The mundane safety warning is the opposite of funny.

jokes.text: Here's where we develop our many reality shows. There's dwarf or midget: America decides, and million dollar fart-off.
jokes.setup: Tour of reality show development
jokes.punchline: Increasingly offensive and lowbrow show concepts
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of reality TV's race to the bottom. The show titles are perfectly awful and believable.

jokes.text: Mother Flippers? What's this show about? / This is a reality program, dear, where we take wives from two very different families and they trade places for a month.
jokes.setup: Marge asks about show title
jokes.punchline: It's exactly the wife-swapping show they're about to be on
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the main plot. The fake title 'Mother Flippers' is a decent pun on 'Wife Swap.'

jokes.text: I already saw that exact same show on another network. / Here, sweetie, have a FOX sweatshirt. / This is an abc sweatshirt. / Yeah, and it zips all the way up.
jokes.setup: Marge points out the show already exists; offered wrong network sweatshirt
jokes.punchline: Guide's weak defense of the ABC sweatshirt
jokes.explanation: Good meta joke about network competition and FOX's cheapness. The zipper defense is perfectly weak.

jokes.text: Spray 'em down!
jokes.setup: Presumably the family is being decontaminated
jokes.punchline: Treating them like livestock or criminals
jokes.explanation: Visual gag that likely works better in animation. The dehumanizing treatment is good satire.

jokes.text: Are you kidding? Take a look at my dvd. They'll never let me near lake havasu again.
jokes.setup: Asked if he's sure about broadcasting intimate moments
jokes.punchline: References previous embarrassing recorded incident
jokes.explanation: Dark implication about what Homer did at Lake Havasu. The specific location makes it funnier.

jokes.text: I'm signing us up for a reality show where we trade you to another family.
jokes.setup: Homer explains what he's doing
jokes.punchline: Casual way of describing trading his wife
jokes.explanation: Homer's matter-of-fact description of 'trading' Marge is appropriately insensitive.

jokes.text: Well, that might be interesting. Or it could damage many, many lives.
jokes.setup: Marge considers the reality show
jokes.punchline: Casual acknowledgment of potential psychological damage
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge response - weighing genuine harm against mild interest. Good understatement.

jokes.text: And if you say yes now, it'll make a great act break.
jokes.setup: Producer continues persuading Marge
jokes.punchline: Breaks fourth wall about TV show structure
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta joke breaking fourth wall about TV production. Shows the producer's complete cynicism.

jokes.text: I'll do it! / Perfect. But could you say it again with more emphasis on the 'I'll'? / I'll do it. / No, no, go back to the first way. / I'll do it. / Wait, I wasn't happy with that. You know what, just do it the way it feels comfortable to you. / I'll do it. I'll do it!
jokes.setup: Marge agrees to do the show
jokes.punchline: Producer's obsessive direction of her spontaneous response
jokes.explanation: Great escalating satire of reality TV's artificial spontaneity. The repeated takes kill the moment's authenticity.

jokes.text: I'll... do... it. / How was that? / We'll get it later.
jokes.setup: Marge tries yet another delivery
jokes.punchline: Producer gives up entirely
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the previous sequence. The producer's defeat is perfectly understated.

jokes.text: Verity, who will be your new mom, is the youngest-ever full professor at yale. / I got my tenure at 28. / Oh, really? Bart, when did you get your tenure? / I got my tenure right here. / Yeah, well I have full tenure.
jokes.setup: Introduction of intellectual replacement mother
jokes.punchline: Bart's crude misunderstanding of 'tenure' followed by Lisa's competitive response
jokes.explanation: Good character work - Bart's crude interpretation vs Lisa's academic competitiveness. The innuendo is slightly cheap but fits Bart.

jokes.text: We better pixelate those. / There aren't enough pixels in the world! / Yeah, just cover it with Ryan seacrest's head.
jokes.setup: Something needs to be censored on TV
jokes.punchline: Escalating to cosmic impossibility, then mundane solution
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from practical to cosmic to mundane. Ryan Seacrest reference is topical and fits the TV satire.

jokes.text: This is Charles. You can call me Charlie. But do not call me late when there are scones about.
jokes.setup: Character introduction with nickname
jokes.punchline: British dad joke about being 'late' vs 'late for scones'
jokes.explanation: Weak dad joke that establishes Charles as trying too hard to be charming. The scones detail adds British specificity.

jokes.text: It's a little british humor. / I'll bet the twist is he's gay.
jokes.setup: Charles explains his joke
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately assumes reality show stereotype
jokes.explanation: Good satire of reality TV tropes and Homer's crude assumptions. Efficient setup/payoff.

jokes.text: Tv. / But dad... / yes, tv-sa? / Forget it.
jokes.setup: Lisa tries to reason with Homer
jokes.punchline: Homer turns her name into TV pun
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay showing Homer's complete TV obsession. Lisa's defeat ('Forget it') is relatable.

jokes.text: Listen up, stonehenge. I made a drawing of the places on Marge you can't touch. Especially the hair.
jokes.setup: Homer warns Charles about boundaries
jokes.punchline: 'Stonehenge' insult and weirdly specific hair protection
jokes.explanation: 'Stonehenge' as an insult is brilliant and random. The hair obsession is perfect Homer/Marge relationship detail.

jokes.text: Oh, you needn't worry. I'm a bit of an elbow man, myself, actually. A bit different, a bit weird. Not sexual.
jokes.setup: Charles tries to reassure Homer
jokes.punchline: Overshares about having an elbow fetish
jokes.explanation: Perfectly awkward overshare. 'A bit different, a bit weird. Not sexual' makes it more uncomfortable.

jokes.text: You take forever to say nothing.
jokes.setup: Charles's verbose, awkward explanation
jokes.punchline: Homer's brutal but accurate assessment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer bluntness cutting through Charles's pretension. Economical and devastatingly accurate.

jokes.text: You know, you don't seem like the money-hungry semi-stripper who's usually on FOX. Why'd you do it?
jokes.setup: Verity assesses Marge's character
jokes.punchline: Casually describes typical FOX reality show participants
jokes.explanation: Sharp FOX self-satire. 'Money-hungry semi-stripper' is brutally specific about reality TV casting.

jokes.text: Well, I thought there was no further way I could humiliate my husband, and then this opportunity came along.
jokes.setup: Verity explains her motivation
jokes.punchline: She's on the show specifically to humiliate her husband
jokes.explanation: Perfectly dark motivation. The casual cruelty is shocking and funny.

jokes.text: Charles hasn't satisfied me in years. / Yeah, that's how I feel about Notre Dame football. Cruel, dusty years.
jokes.setup: Verity shares sexual frustration
jokes.punchline: Marge relates it to football disappointment
jokes.explanation: Another perfect Marge misunderstanding. 'Cruel, dusty years' is poetic and perfectly innocent.

jokes.text: So, Charles, what do you do for a living? / Well, I'm an office manager, and, no, I didn't even get a promotion today, again. So, go on, go ahead. Disembowel me with your pointy, pointy words.
jokes.setup: Marge asks innocent question about Charles's job
jokes.punchline: Charles pre-emptively defends against criticism with dramatic language
jokes.explanation: Great character establishment. Charles's self-defeating attitude and 'pointy, pointy words' is perfectly pathetic.

jokes.text: Really? You manage a whole office? / Yes. Yes. Yes I do, actually. / Well, that's very impressive.
jokes.setup: Marge shows genuine interest in Charles's job
jokes.punchline: Charles is shocked by basic human kindness
jokes.explanation: Sweet moment showing Marge's kindness vs Charles's low self-esteem. His amazement at basic encouragement is touching and funny.

jokes.text: Does that include the people and the furniture? / Yeah, and I decide where the christmas party's held. Actually, it's decided by committee, but, you know, I choose the committee.
jokes.setup: Marge asks about scope of management
jokes.punchline: Charles inflates then deflates his authority
jokes.explanation: Great escalation showing Charles building himself up then immediately backing down. The committee logic is perfectly bureaucratic.

jokes.text: Hello. / I don't, I don't choose the committee as such, but, you know, I choose where they meet. This year I am thinking of conference room 'C.'
jokes.setup: Charles continues inflating his minimal authority
jokes.punchline: Further reduction to just choosing meeting room
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of Charles's pathetic authority inflation. Conference room 'C' is perfectly mundane.

jokes.text: That leaves 'a' and 'b' available for overflow. / Well done!
jokes.setup: Charles explains conference room logistics
jokes.punchline: Marge's enthusiastic praise for basic room allocation
jokes.explanation: Marge's genuine enthusiasm for Charles's trivial accomplishment is sweet. Shows her nurturing nature.

jokes.text: Bart, I told you no television till you do the dishes. / And I do not call that doing the dishes.
jokes.setup: Verity enforces discipline on Bart
jokes.punchline: Implies Bart did something ridiculous that counts as 'doing dishes'
jokes.explanation: Visual gag that doesn't translate well to transcript. Likely shows Bart's creative interpretation of 'doing dishes.'

jokes.text: well, my mom says why bother punishing me, I never learn.
jokes.setup: Bart responds to discipline
jokes.punchline: Casually admits his mother has given up on him
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark insight into Marge's parenting defeat. Bart's casual delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Disgusting. Young man, I'm going to turn that cartoon into homework!
jokes.setup: Verity is appalled by what Bart's watching
jokes.punchline: Academic punishment that turns entertainment into education
jokes.explanation: Good character-appropriate punishment from the academic. The threat is both creative and cruel to a child.

jokes.text: Help me, cameraman. / I let you smoke a cigarette in my bedroom. / That was a joint. And I have a name. It's Doug.
jokes.setup: Bart appeals to cameraman for help
jokes.punchline: Reveals inappropriate relationship and drug use
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation revealing unprofessional cameraman behavior. 'And I have a name. It's Doug' is perfect wounded dignity.

jokes.text: 'Then itchy used an ice cream scoop to scoop out scratchy's heart and make it a sundae. The end.' Now, Homer, would you care to give your report on CSI: Miami?
jokes.setup: Verity forces educational analysis of violent cartoon
jokes.punchline: Homer gets assigned to analyze equally lowbrow TV show
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Verity's academic approach. The Itchy & Scratchy violence description is appropriately graphic.

jokes.text: 'there's this guy that got killed. I think it was in Miami. So CSI: Miami investigated-ed it. Then a family said how much they loved the olive garden. Then I fell asleep. When I woke up, Letterman was talking to Alias.'
jokes.setup: Homer gives his TV analysis
jokes.punchline: Completely incoherent summary mixing shows and commercials
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer TV watching experience. The confusion of shows, commercials, and falling asleep is brilliantly realistic.

jokes.text: That's wonderful. Indeed. Now, if you'll excuse me, I have a mandarin chinese lesson. Xie xie. Zai jian.
jokes.setup: Verity praises Homer's terrible report
jokes.punchline: Shows off with Chinese language skills
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Verity's intellectual pretension. The Chinese phrases add authentic detail.

jokes.text: He's a very impressive boy. / The apple doesn't fall far from the tree. / A tree that's been alone for far too long.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Bart's family resemblance
jokes.punchline: Charles turns it into self-pity about loneliness
jokes.explanation: Good metaphor extension. Charles turning a casual saying into personal loneliness is perfect character work.

jokes.text: I don't get it. / Are you saying you're the tree? / Maybe. / Are my roots showing? / Wordplay, brilliant.
jokes.setup: Marge doesn't understand Charles's metaphor
jokes.punchline: Charles extends tree metaphor awkwardly; praiseworthy wordplay
jokes.explanation: Awkward extended metaphor that shows Charles trying too hard. His self-praise ('brilliant') makes it cringier.

jokes.text: Would you like to hear a joke? / As long as it's not a knock-knock joke. I always ruin them by saying, 'come in.'
jokes.setup: Charles offers to tell a joke
jokes.punchline: Marge's innocent misunderstanding of knock-knock joke format
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge innocence. Her literal interpretation of 'knock-knock' is both sweet and funny.

jokes.text: You see, this man, he finds a magic lamp, and a genie comes out. / That's funny. / That's just the setup. / Well, you've set me up, for laughs down the road. / So the genie says, 'I'll give you three wishes, but whatever you get, your wife gets double.' / Okay? Remember that. / How nice for her.
jokes.setup: Charles tells classic genie joke setup
jokes.punchline: Marge's innocent appreciation of wife getting double
jokes.explanation: Standard joke setup with Marge's innocent reaction. Her genuine happiness for the wife is sweet.

jokes.text: Now, here's the humor. The man says, 'I want a new car. The genie says, 'your wife gets two.' Okay, remember the double? The man says, 'I want a new house.' So the genie says, 'your wife gets two.' So the man says... 'beat me half to death.'
jokes.setup: Standard progression of genie joke
jokes.punchline: Dark punchline about domestic violence
jokes.explanation: Classic but problematic joke. The domestic violence punchline is dated and cheap.

jokes.text: It's funny. Don't you get it? 'Cause she gets double, so if he gets beaten half to death, the wife would be beaten to death.
jokes.setup: Charles explains the joke
jokes.punchline: Makes the dark humor even more explicit and uncomfortable
jokes.explanation: Awkward joke explanation that makes it worse. Good character work showing Charles's social ineptness.

jokes.text: That sounds horrible. / I agree, terrible. Offensive towards women. Little amusing, don't you think? / No! / Me, either. Spousal abuse. It's, uh, it's a real problem. It's not funny. It's tragic, if anything so...
jokes.setup: Marge rejects the joke
jokes.punchline: Charles's backpedaling becomes increasingly awkward
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy as Charles desperately tries to recover. His awkward social awareness lecture is perfectly uncomfortable.

jokes.text: well, I liked the genie part. That was fun. / You think so? / Yes, I could really picture him. With curly shoes and smoke all over the place.
jokes.setup: Marge finds something positive to say
jokes.punchline: Her innocent visualization of the genie
jokes.explanation: Sweet Marge moment finding something kind to say. 'Curly shoes and smoke' is perfectly innocent imagery.

jokes.text: You're a born storyteller. / Well, yeah, I suppose I am, yeah. / Yeah, not a murderer. / No.
jokes.setup: Marge compliments Charles's storytelling
jokes.punchline: Charles's weird non-sequitur about not being a murderer
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly random and dark. The unprompted 'not a murderer' comment is perfectly unsettling.

jokes.text: Would you like to hear another joke? / I sure would. / Let me just get us some iced tea.
jokes.setup: Charles offers another joke
jokes.punchline: Marge's enthusiasm despite the previous disaster
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Marge's persistent kindness. Sets up Charles's growing infatuation.

jokes.text: One more kind word from her and I am completely smitten.
jokes.setup: Charles reacts to Marge's kindness
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's falling for basic human decency
jokes.explanation: Good character development. Shows how starved Charles is for basic kindness that he mistakes it for romance.

jokes.text: Your toaster takes bagels. How ritzy. / And there we have it.
jokes.setup: Marge makes innocent comment about toaster
jokes.punchline: Charles confirms this is the compliment that makes him fall for her
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback execution. The mundane toaster comment being the final straw is perfectly absurd and pathetic.

jokes.text: what's that delicious smell? / I'm burning all your underwear.
jokes.setup: Homer notices pleasant smell
jokes.punchline: It's his underwear being burned
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection. The casual destruction of Homer's belongings is both shocking and funny.

jokes.text: what's with the kimono? 'Are we having Lachoy?' No Marge, I was just sitting around drinking Vermouth and contemplating how, just when you're drowning in a pit of despair, life can throw you a beautiful blue life preserver.
jokes.setup: Marge asks about Charles's outfit choice
jokes.punchline: Charles's dramatic metaphor about finding hope
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Charles's romantic delusion. The elaborate metaphor for Marge's basic kindness is perfectly overwrought.

jokes.text: I thought life preservers only came in orange. Because wouldn't it be hard to see blue in the ocean?
jokes.setup: Charles uses blue life preserver metaphor
jokes.punchline: Marge's practical concern about visibility
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge response. Her practical interruption of Charles's romantic metaphor is brilliantly deflating.

jokes.text: Pa, pa, pa, pa, pa. No logic. Not tonight.
jokes.setup: Marge applies logic to his metaphor
jokes.punchline: Charles dramatically rejects rational thought
jokes.explanation: Good character work. Charles's rejection of logic in favor of romance is perfectly pretentious and pathetic.

jokes.text: How long do we have to stay here? / Till I'm sick of drinking these, and I'm never sick of drinkin' these.
jokes.setup: Bart wants to know duration of their stay
jokes.punchline: Homer's alcohol-dependent timeline
jokes.explanation: Good Homer characterization. His priorities being entirely alcohol-focused is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Did I tell you I'm a bit of a songwriter? Words and music, hold the applause. I wrote this song for a woman. You. / What an odd thing for a man who's not interested in me to do. / Yes. Not interested. Let me just breathe your scent for a moment before I play.
jokes.setup: Charles reveals song; claims no romantic interest
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts himself with creepy behavior
jokes.explanation: Excellent cringe comedy. Charles's immediate contradiction and the 'breathe your scent' line is perfectly inappropriate.

jokes.text: Lady when you came to me, I was feeling blue blue just like your hair, you see blue just like the moon but only when the moon is blue and not when it is cream
jokes.setup: Charles performs his romantic song
jokes.punchline: Terrible lyrics with confused metaphors
jokes.explanation: Good cringe songwriting. The confused moon metaphor and poor rhyme scheme is authentically bad amateur songwriting.

jokes.text: and now that you are here with me I am in a dream oh, Yeah, Marge, your dreams can come true.
jokes.setup: Charles continues his terrible song
jokes.punchline: Homer interrupts with TV commercial-style encouragement
jokes.explanation: Good interruption breaking romantic tension. Homer's commercial-speak is appropriately tone-deaf.

jokes.text: Lady, when you go away I feel like I could die not like dye like your hair is dyed but die like Lady Di and not Like Di like her name is Di but die like when she died.
jokes.setup: Charles continues his song with death metaphor
jokes.punchline: Terrible pun explanation involving Princess Diana
jokes.explanation: Intentionally terrible songwriting. The over-explanation of the die/dye pun and tasteless Diana reference is perfectly awful.

jokes.text: But lady just like lady di be my princess tonight but don't die don't die. No way.
jokes.setup: Charles continues Princess Diana metaphor
jokes.punchline: Awkward plea for her not to die
jokes.explanation: Continuation of terrible song. The 'don't die' plea is appropriately awkward conclusion.

jokes.text: That song was very nice. / How'd you think up so many rhyming words?
jokes.setup: Marge politely compliments awful song
jokes.punchline: Her innocent question about the terrible wordplay
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge politeness. Her innocent appreciation of the terrible rhymes is both sweet and funny.

jokes.text: Marge, I love you. And I can tell from your basic level of courtesy that you love me, too.
jokes.setup: Charles declares his love
jokes.punchline: Misinterprets politeness as romantic interest
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment. 'Basic level of courtesy' as evidence of love is perfectly pathetic and delusional.

jokes.text: Listen, Charles, I don't want to hurt your feelings... / because you love me, right? Admit it. We were born to fall into each others' arms on reality tv.
jokes.setup: Marge tries to let Charles down gently
jokes.punchline: Charles interprets kindness as more evidence; mentions reality TV
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Charles's delusion. The 'born to fall into each other's arms on reality TV' is perfectly artificial.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, but I love Homer.
jokes.setup: Marge needs to reject Charles clearly
jokes.punchline: Simple, direct statement of her actual feelings
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge moment. Her simple directness cuts through all of Charles's delusion.

jokes.text: Of course you do. He's a real man, not like me, a miserable toad under the thumb of the biggest bitch this side of the Westminster Kennel Club.
jokes.setup: Charles accepts Marge's rejection
jokes.punchline: Immediately launches into vicious attack on his wife
jokes.explanation: Dark turn showing Charles's true bitterness. The Westminster Kennel Club comparison is specific and vicious.

jokes.text: Charles, please, your son might see this. / He's not my son. His real father is either the pool man or my wife's lover. They're the main two candidates I've narrowed it down to. Pretty sure.
jokes.setup: Marge tries to protect the child
jokes.punchline: Charles reveals devastating family secrets casually
jokes.explanation: Shocking escalation of family dysfunction. The casual 'Pretty sure' makes it even more disturbing and funny.

jokes.text: Elle m'a fait passer du temps avec les enfants. Et le temps pour la télévision est devenu un temps de dialogue.
jokes.setup: Homer describing his ordeal with Verity
jokes.punchline: His worst complaint is family time replacing TV time
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer priorities - quality time with family is torture compared to TV watching.
